tree.title: Login
tree.text:
# Login

Users to get access to application by being added to the Okta user group . SSO Login: All Bain users (added to the above group) have SSO login enabled. Users have access to only limited case(s) which will be controlled by the admin users. Initially this is controlled by running SQL queries to add/modify user access with Admin page to be added post MVP. On Login user will land on Home page with a list of cases accessible to the user or can create a new case. Below is a tentative landing page design

The screen displays the home page after a successful login, showing a dashboard with a list of accessible companies for the user. The left sidebar includes navigation options such as 'All companies', 'My companies', 'Bookmarked companies', and 'Settings', along with a 'Create company' button. The main area presents a grid of company cards, each displaying the company name, industry, revenue, employee count, and last update date. Users can filter companies by industry, revenue, and headcount using dropdown menus at the top. This interface helps users manage and access detailed information about various companies within the platform.

tree.title: Site Navigation
tree.text:
# Site Navigation

## Navigation Bar

The navigation bar displayed is part of the platform's site navigation, showing various sections and options available to the user after completing the tool setup process. Key sections include 'Explore & adjust input data' with options like 'Employee', 'GL', and 'Aggregated View', and 'Assessments' which further divides into 'Benchmarking', 'Shared Services', 'Automation', and 'Integrated View', each with sub-options such as 'Data upload', 'Explore benchmark', 'Assumptions', and 'Output'. At the bottom, the 'Tool setup' menu provides access to 'Company profile', 'Assessment & functional taxonomy', and 'Upload client data'. This navigation bar helps users efficiently access different parts of the platform based on their selected assessments.

Navigation bar is case specific. It will display only the options depending on the assessments selected by the user.

At the bottom of the navigation bar is the “Tool Setup” menu. Users can use these options to go to the setup pages at any time during the analysis.

When a user start creating a case for the first time, they will not see any navigation bar. The navigation is made visible only after the user has completed the tool setup process.

tree.title: Company Overview
tree.text:
# Company Overview

## Purpose

This is the Landing page for the user when starting a new case. This will collect high level info regarding the company

The screen displays the 'Company Overview' section for creating a new company profile within the platform. It features a form titled 'Company profile' with fields for entering essential company information such as Company Name, Industry, Company Revenue, Total number of Head Count, Currency, and Exchange rate. A navigation bar at the top outlines the workflow steps: Create company profile, Select assessment and functional taxonomy, Upload client data, and Upload benchmarks. A 'Next stage' button is present but currently disabled, indicating that the form must be completed before proceeding. This screen helps users input foundational business details to initiate a new case.

## Validations

- All fields are mandatory
- No of employees cannot be negative and should only input numbers
- Exchange rate should be >0
- Company revenue should be >0

## Click actions

### Abandon company

Any details entered are removed and user is redirected to Home page. No new case is created

### Next Stage

Check all validations are met. A new case is created in the database. User is directed to the assessment selection page

## Form actions

Exchange rate will be populated from a standard table in the DB. The exchange rate data will be updated on a quarterly basis by the COE. However, the user has rights to update the exchange rate manually.

tree.title: Assessment Selection and setup
tree.text:
# Assessment Selection and setup

## Purpose

This page captures the assessments that the user wants to run

The screen displays the 'Assessment Selection and Setup' page, where users can choose assessments and configure business functions for analysis. It includes checkboxes for selecting different assessments such as 'Top-down benchmarking' and 'Shared Services Opportunity Assessment.' Below, a table lists business functions like Finance, HR, and IT, with options to rename and select levels for Benchmarking, Shared Services, and Automation. Users can add new business functions using the 'Add business function' button. This page facilitates the setup of assessments by allowing users to customize and select relevant business functions for their analysis.

## Page actions

### Assessment selection

Users can select one or more assessments. Based on the assessment selected, business functions displayed would vary.

Ex: If a user selects only Benchmarking and “Operations” function is not present for it , then it will not be shown in the list

### Key note

### Functions and granularity level selection

- Relevant functions to be displayed based on assessments selected
- For all assessments “L2” to be selected by default
- In case a selected level is not applicable for an assessment, when a user selects that level, they will get an error immediately below the dropdown saying “No benchmarks available”
- All functions to be assorted as given in figma. Any new custom fields added by user will be added at the bottom of the list.

### Feature /Processes/Activities selection page

- Once the features are selected, user moves to the next page to select L2 and L3 layer. Only the functions selected in previous pages are displayed here.
- Users can click on the “Edit” button to display the list of L2 levels for the Function.
- L2 levels will be displayed as editable text that the user can easily rename them.
- L3 levels will be shown as a slidedown when a L2 level is clicked upon. These will also be editable directly
- Users can add both custom L2 and L3 levels but if they define a L2 level they are responsible to define the lower L3 hierarchy for the same

The Feature/Processes/Activities selection page displays a list of business functions such as Finance, HR, IT, Legal, Facility Management, Marketing, and Operations. Each function has an 'Edit' button, allowing users to modify the taxonomy of each business function. The 'Reset to default' button is available but currently disabled. The page facilitates the review and customization of the taxonomy for each business function, enabling users to tailor the hierarchy to their specific needs. A 'Next stage' button is visible at the bottom, indicating progression to the subsequent step in the workflow.

The 'Edit Finance' screen allows users to manage Processes (L2) and Activities (L3) within the finance function. Users can rename, add, or delete entries. The interface displays a list of L2 processes such as 'Financial Planning & Analysis' and 'Control / Internal Audit,' with expandable sections to reveal associated L3 activities like 'Policies & Procedures' and 'Audit.' Each entry has an edit icon for renaming and a delete icon for removal. A button labeled 'Add activity' enables users to introduce new activities. The screen includes 'Cancel' and 'Save & Update' buttons to discard or save changes, respectively, facilitating efficient customization of financial processes.

### Template column selection page

- Users will select the template they want to input data. The options are dependent on the assessment selected. HRIS and GL files are present for all assessments but a third option appears for Outsourced files if a user selects SSOI as one of the assessments.
- The user can then select the relevant columns for which they plan to run the assessments

The Template Column Selection page allows users to choose templates for data input based on the selected assessment. Visible are options for 'HR Baseline' and 'GL Baseline' templates, each with a preview section listing field names such as 'Unique ID' and 'Employee type' for HR, and 'Functional Allocation' for GL. Users can add fields using the 'Add field' button. This screen facilitates the customization of data templates, enabling users to select relevant columns for running assessments, particularly when dealing with HRIS, GL, and potentially Outsourced files if SSOI is selected.

### Upload templates

- Users will upload the Baseline data from this page. The columns included in the files are dynamic as they are dependent on the selections made by the user in previous step.

The screen displays the 'Upload templates' page, where users can upload baseline data files for HR and GL categories. Each section provides an option to download a CSV template and a designated area to drag and drop files for upload. The page instructs users to upload CSV files no larger than 20MB and includes a link to an FAQ for additional guidance. This interface facilitates the data upload process by allowing users to prepare and submit their baseline data, which is essential for subsequent analysis and benchmarking tasks.

### Upload validations

Any files uploaded by the user go through multiple validations before the data is inserted in the database.

### Columns check

The columns in the uploaded file are checked against the list of columns selected in previous steps. In case of any missing columns or additional columns, an error is displayed on the screen which lists the missing/additional columns. Users need to update their files and reupload the files OR they can choose to add/remove columns from the previous page to match the column list of their file

### File specific validations

Headcount/Outsource:

1. Employee ID should be unique: Employee ID is not a mandatory field but if included the IDs should be unique
2. FTE allocation should be <= 100% : Capacity allocation of any employee cannot be more than 100%

GL:

1. In case any validations fail, then the user is prompted regarding errors. They can download the error file which provides a list of error against each row specifying the validation which failed for that particular row. User can then fix the file and upload the file again after removing the error column.

### Preview templates

Once the validations succeed, user is shown a “read-only” preview of the uploaded file so they can cross check that the file has been uploaded successfully and correctly.

# Data upload page

## Purpose

The Data Upload screen allows users to upload the essential input files (such as HR Baseline and GL data) that provide the base client data for all the assessments

## Core Functional Requirements

The image displays the data upload page for HR and GL Baseline files within the platform. It features two main sections: one for HR Baseline and another for GL Baseline. Each section includes a 'Download template' button, allowing users to download sample files, and a drag-and-drop area for uploading CSV files up to 20MB. The page provides guidance on uploading data and links to FAQs for additional support. This screen facilitates the process of uploading necessary baseline data files, ensuring users can easily manage and input their data into the system.

### File Upload Functionality

### Upload Types Supported

- HR Baseline File
- GL baseline file
  Supported Formats:xlsx

### File Template Download

Button to download sample/template files for:

- HR Baseline
- GL Baseline

### File Validation on Upload

- Check required columns exist (e.g., Employee ID, Function, Labor Cost, % Allocation)
- Check for missing values in mandatory fields
- Check if numeric columns contain valid numbers
- A singe excel file to be downloaded as output with a list of errors found in the template. User will need to fix the issues and then reupload

## Preview

- On successful validation, a preview of the uploaded file to be shown in a table.
- NO editing option to be provided at this stage
- User will have an option to reupload file in case of any issues
- Re-upload button to replace file

## Integration with Backend

### On successful upload

Data is stored in case specific tables with no interaction with any other case data

tree.title: Benchmarking
tree.text:
# Benchmarking

## Tool Overview

Benchmarking assessment tool is used to run ben

## Screens

The Benchmarking assessment tool section will have three pages:

- Benchmark Upload
- Explore Benchmarks
- Output

## Benchmark Upload

This page is required to upload the benchmarking data to be used for the assessment.

The Benchmark Upload screen facilitates the uploading of benchmarking data for assessments. It features a 'Download Template' button, allowing users to download a preformatted Excel template. Users can upload their data by dragging and dropping a CSV file into the designated area, with a file size limit of 20MB. The page includes a link to an FAQ for additional guidance. This screen is part of the workflow for uploading benchmark data, which is essential for comparing key performance indicators (KPIs) across different taxonomy levels.

### Download Template

On clicking the “Download Template” button, an excel file is downloaded in the format shown below.

1. The Template is prefilled with the taxonomy levels selected by the user on the Assessment & Taxonomy selection page.
2. Any functions/processes/activities that have been modified or newly added by the user will be highlighted using a different font color
3. To define the benchmark values for the same taxonomy level for different sets, users need to duplicate the rows
4. For each level , the first row will be considered as the primary/default peer set for that combination. The default peer sets can be changed later from the “Explore Benchmarks” page

Currently the tool offers the following 6 KPIs for comparison:

### Overall cost (needs GL)

1. Cost of X (for L1 function, L2 process or L3 activities) as a % of revenue
2. Cost of X per employee

### Number of employees (needs HC)

1. # of employees in X as a % of total employees
2. # of employees in X per $1B revenue

### Personnel cost (needs GL or HC; consider comment in sticker in GL columns slide)

1. Personnel cost of X as a % of revenue
2. Personnel cost of X per employee

### Validations

Following validations are performed on the benchmarking data:

### KPI values

Checks for the standard KPIs and if any unknown KPIs are present in the data then the above 6

### Data type mismatch

% values provided for KPIs with % metrics and numbers for numerical metrics

### Mandatory fields

Checks that none of the mandatory fields should be blank Validations are performed on the uploaded benchmarks and error file generated similar to the Baseline error file. User would then need to fix the errors and reupload the data

### Benchmark preview

If the file validation is successful, a preview of the updated data is displayed to the user. This is read-only and no editing can be done in this preview. In case the user wants to update the data , they can use the “X” button on top right corner of the preview screen . Alternatively, user can also navigate to the “Explore Benchmarks” page to edit the data.

### Benchmark Summary preview

Using the toggle on the preview page, user can switch the preview page to a summary view. This view shows the count of benchmarks available for each taxonomy level for each KPI. Any taxonomy elements which are present in the baseline data but there is no benchmark provided for them in the uploaded file are highlighted in different color.

Once the user verifies the uploaded benchmark data, they can click on the “Next” button to look at the final analysis.

The Benchmark Summary preview screen displays a detailed table summarizing the uploaded benchmark data from the file 'Benchmarks_7.15.2025.csv'. The table is organized by taxonomy levels, including Function (L1), Process (L2), and Sub-process (L3), and provides metrics such as Overall Cost, Number of Employees, and Personnel Cost. Each metric is further broken down into specific KPIs like 'Cost as % of Revenue' and 'Personnel cost per employee'. Users can verify the benchmark data, with any discrepancies highlighted, before proceeding to the final analysis by clicking the 'Next stage' button.

## Explore benchmarks

The 'Explore benchmarks' screen displays an editable table for managing benchmark data within a business case. The table includes columns for Function, Process, Sub-process, Metric, Source, Industry, Region, Revenue, FTE, and various performance metrics such as Top Quartile and Median. Users can add new benchmarks using the 'Add benchmarks' button, which supports Excel uploads for appending data. The 'Reset to default' option allows reverting changes to the last saved state. Each row can be expanded to show detailed metrics, and users can delete benchmarks using the '-' icon. This interface facilitates detailed analysis and customization of benchmark data specific to the current case.

This page provides an Editable table for the Benchmark data.

This view will also show a data views and a summary view, but the summary view will not be editable.

In the data view, users can edit existing benchmark values , add new benchmarks and delete benchmarks. It should be noted that this is specific to the current case and changes do not have any impact to benchmark data being used for any other case.

“Reset to Default” functionality on the top will reset the page to its last saved status of the data. The “-“ sign can be used to delete a benchmark from the data.

In order to add new data , user can click on the “Add benchmarks” button. This is similar to the upload page in the Tool Setup and the user will need to upload new benchmarks data via an excel upload. It should be noted that the excel should contain only the new data rows as this is an append functionality. The old data will not be overwritten by this file upload. The validations for the file will remain safe. In case of any duplicates, the system will remove them by itself and provide a notification to the user.

In case of any error, a benchmark failed status will be shown and error file can be downloaded.

The 'Explore benchmarks' screen displays an interface for adding new benchmark data via a CSV file upload. A modal window titled 'Add benchmarks' is open, prompting users to upload a CSV file no larger than 20MB. Users can download a template for the CSV file and either click or drag files into the designated upload area. The main table in the background shows various benchmark categories such as Function, Process, and Performance metrics, which users can edit, add to, or delete from. The 'Reset to Default' button allows users to revert changes to the last saved state. This screen facilitates the management and updating of benchmark data specific to the current case.

The 'Explore benchmarks' screen displays an editable table for managing benchmark data specific to the current case. Users can view and edit existing benchmarks, add new ones, or delete entries using the '-' sign. The table includes columns for function, process, sub-process, metrics, and performance indicators such as median and quartile values. Notifications at the top confirm successful uploads and highlight skipped duplicates. The 'Add benchmarks' button allows users to append new data via an Excel upload, while 'Reset to default' reverts changes to the last saved state. This interface facilitates detailed analysis and management of benchmark data.

## Output

The image displays the 'Analysis results' section of the Benchmarking Output page, focusing on the Detailed View. It shows a table with columns for Function, Process, Sub-process, Metric, Baseline Value, and Gap to Benchmark percentages (Top Quartile, Median, Bottom Quartile). The table provides detailed benchmarking data for various financial processes, such as 'Invoice to Cash' and 'Tax', with metrics like 'Cost as % revenue'. Users can compare their baseline values against benchmarks to identify savings potential and peer sets. The Detailed View toggle is visible, indicating the current view mode.

Output page provides the benchmarking comparison between the client values and benchmarks uploaded. It contains two views: Summary and Detail view

### Calculations for Client value

- Cost as % revenue :
- Cost of X taxonomy per employee
- # of employees in taxonomy X as as % of total employees
- # of employees in X per $1B revenue
- Personnel cost of X as a % of revenue
- Personnel cost of X per employee

### Summary view

Summary view shows the analysis data for one KPI and peer set at a time. When the user first lands on the page , the view shows a “default” scenario with the taxonomy selected by the user during Tool Setup at the highest granularity. The default metric and peer set are selected. User can chose to change the granularity for each function individually .

### Detailed View

The Detailed view shows the analysis data for all the KPIs available for each taxonomy level but only for one peer set. User can choose to change the peer set at a row level. This view also shows the calculated client value for each KPI.

“Gap to Benchmark” : it should be noted that both the views do not show the actual benchmarking values but only the % gap of the client’s KPI value to the benchmarking value. The user can chose which quartile they want to compare to (Top/Median/Bottom/Custom value) and the savings potential is calculated corresponding to the selected quartile. The selected quartile is highlighted in a “purple” color.

In the “Gap to benchmark” column, a blank value means that no benchmark is available for that quartile. A “-“ means that the client value is already better than the quartile benchmark.

### Saving a Scenario

User can create new scenarios or save old ones (except Default, which would always show the original values). The Save functionality is an event based Save where the scenario gets saved when a user creates a new scenario, switches to a new scenario or moves to a different tab.

nodes.text:
### Summary view

Summary view shows the analysis data for one KPI and peer set at a time. When the user first lands on the page , the view shows a “default” scenario with the taxonomy selected by the user during Tool Setup at the highest granularity. The default metric and peer set are selected. User can chose to change the granularity for each function individually .

nodes.title: Detailed View
nodes.text:
### Detailed View

The Detailed view shows the analysis data for all the KPIs available for each taxonomy level but only for one peer set. User can choose to change the peer set at a row level. This view also shows the calculated client value for each KPI.

“Gap to Benchmark” : it should be noted that both the views do not show the actual benchmarking values but only the % gap of the client’s KPI value to the benchmarking value. The user can chose which quartile they want to compare to (Top/Median/Bottom/Custom value) and the savings potential is calculated corresponding to the selected quartile. The selected quartile is highlighted in a “purple” color.

In the “Gap to benchmark” column, a blank value means that no benchmark is available for that quartile. A “-“ means that the client value is already better than the quartile benchmark.

tree.title: Shared Services
tree.text:
# Shared Services

## Tool Overview

The Shared Services Opportunity Indicator (SSOI) is a strategic analysis tool designed to help organizations evaluate, model, and optimize their shared services footprint across business functions such as Finance and HR etc... It supports data-driven decisions for transitioning operations into centralized shared service centers and enables users to simulate multiple future-state scenarios, estimate cost savings, and understand organizational impact.

## Functional Objectives

The tool enables users to:

- Analyze the current state of functional personnel distribution.
- Define and apply benchmark-driven target models.
- Simulate scenarios for shared services adoption.
- Estimate financial impact and FTE movement.
- Generate comparative outputs to aid executive decision-making.

## Screens

Shared Services tool can be divided into three major sections:

### Overall Assumptions

- SSOI Benchmarks
- Site Selection
- Resource allocation
- Site allocation

### One off cost assumptions

- Transition costs
- Severance costs
- Other one-off costs

### Output

- Savings and FTE evolution
- Annual run-rate savings
- FTE impact overall view
- FTE shifts
- One-time cost overview
- Calendar view
- All data table

## Overall assumptions

The SSOI Assumptions landing page provides an overview of critical and one-off cost assumptions necessary for Shared Services projections. It features sections for 'Overall assumptions' and 'One-off cost assumptions,' each containing interactive cards such as 'Benchmark,' 'Site selection,' 'Site allocation,' and placeholders for additional screens. Each card includes a brief description and a navigation arrow, guiding users to detailed input pages. This screen helps users define baseline inputs like labor cost, site strategy, and cost estimates, forming the foundation for further analysis and decision-making in the platform.

This is the main landing page for the SSOI tool. Inputs in the Benchmarks, Site selection and Site allocation pages are compulsory inputs as they provide critical data for the tool analysis.

### SSOI Benchmarks

Benchmarks page is the first page in the SSOI Assumptions module. It is a compulsory input page which a user needs to interact with.

The SSOI Benchmarks page displays a table for managing benchmark data and process improvement metrics within the SSOI Assumptions module. The table includes columns for 'Function/Process/Sub-Process', 'SSC Full Potential', 'SSC Benchmark' with Local, Regional, and Global percentages, and 'Customize Site Allocation' with toggle switches. The 'Process Improvement' section shows 'Process Improv. Max Potential', 'Client Starting Point', and 'Process Improv. Applied'. Users can edit benchmark values, ensuring the sum of Local, Regional, and Global equals 100%. The 'Reset to Default' option allows reverting changes. This page is essential for inputting and adjusting benchmark data for analysis.

The first columns is the “Taxonomy” column which will show the L1/L2/L3 in a tree data structure.

The column will only show the taxonomy levels selected by the user for the SSOI analysis even if benchmark data is available in the database for other levels. Benchmark data will be pre uploaded by COE team from backend. This is a common data for all cases, however case specific changes can be made by users.

The page has two sections - Benchmark data and Process Improvement data.

Benchmark section shows the actual values from the Benchmark file. These values are editable but validation will be done that the sum of Local, Regional and Global columns always should be equal to 100%. If a user has defined a custom taxonomy , then the row is shown as empty and the cells are highlighted with red borders

For the PI section, the “client starting point” is set to 3 by default. This point can be changed by the user with range from 1-5 (1 being the least mature and 5 – most mature)

“Enabled Site Allocation” column is a toggle column which can be used to provide details about a certain site. If a user toggles this ON for any site then the site gets displayed in the Resource allocation page where further details can be provided.

If a user attempts to leave page without resolving any error, following error should be shown:

The screenshot displays an error message dialog indicating that the percentages for Local, Regional, and Global must sum to 100%. It prompts the user to adjust the numbers accordingly. The dialog includes a 'See error info' link for more details, and two buttons: 'Copy error info' for copying the error details, and 'Got it!' to acknowledge the message. This screen is part of the SSOI Benchmarks page, ensuring users correct input errors before proceeding, maintaining data integrity in the benchmarking process.

If they still choose to Save then we show a warning sign on the Assumptions landing page.

On clicking Got It, user does not navigate away, they still need to stay on the page to fix the issue.

“Reset to Default” : This resets the benchmark to its last saved status. Eevery row also has individual “Rest to default” icons, clicking on these icons resets any changes made to that row.

### Site Selection

The 'Site Selection' screen displays a table where users can manage labor costs for various sites. It includes columns for 'Country', 'SSC Sites', 'Type of SSC', 'Current FTEs', 'Average fully loaded cost ($k) / FTE', and 'Labor cost for new FTEs ($k) / FTE'. Users can view the average labor costs, which are read-only, and propose new labor costs for each site. The screen also features an 'Add location' button for creating new sites. The interface supports scenario selection, with options to rename or create new scenarios, facilitating detailed cost management and compliance with benchmarking standards.

The 'Site Selection' screen displays a data table where users can manage labor costs for various SSC sites. Key columns include 'Country', 'SSC Sites', 'Type of SSC', 'Current FTEs', and 'Average fully loaded cost ($K) / FTE'. Users can propose new labor costs for different departments such as Finance, HR, IT, and more, under 'Labor Cost for new FTEs ($K) / FTE'. The table allows users to expand or collapse department details. The scenario dropdown at the top lets users select different scenarios, and options to rename or create new scenarios are available. This screen aids in defining and adjusting labor costs at site levels for benchmarking purposes.

“Site Selection” screen can be used by the users to define the cost of labor at each site. They can define this at a site level or drill down to the lowest granularity level and define the labor cost at that level.

The default view shows the average labor cost for each site calculated from the baseline data. The “Average labor cost” is a read only column and cannot be edited by the user as it is the actual average labor cost from baseline data. However, user can propose a new labor cost for each site in the “Labor cost for new FTEs” column.

Users can also propose creation of new sites to comply with the benchmarking standards. However, this view is connected to the benchmarking view. If there is at least one % >0 in local or regional or global, user must have at least one equivalent site (e.g., one local if at least one process as a >0 in the previous, etc.). An error message would be shown to the user if the above condition is not met.

The 'Site Selection' screen allows users to manage labor costs for various sites. It displays a table with columns for 'Country', 'SSC Sites', 'Type of SSC', 'Current FTEs', 'Average fully loaded cost ($K) / FTE', and 'Labor cost for new FTEs ($K) / FTE'. Users can view average labor costs, which are read-only, and propose new labor costs for each site. The interface includes options to add new locations and manage scenarios, such as 'Restructuring, Q3 2025'. The screen supports compliance with benchmarking standards by allowing the creation of new sites if necessary.

### Resource allocation

The screen displays the 'Resource Allocation' interface within the 'Overall Assumptions' section, allowing users to adjust resource allocations by site. It features a table with columns for site code, type of site, baseline, target, and balance. Users can modify allocations in increments of 0.1, ensuring the total matches the full-time equivalent (FTE) count. A warning message highlights overstaffed or understaffed categories in red, prompting adjustments. The interface includes options to rename or create new scenarios, and a reset button to revert changes. The balance column shows discrepancies that need correction to maintain allocation consistency.

This view is an optional view. This is available only if the user selects the toggle in the Benchmarking view. It allows the user to change the resource allocation by site. It can be done only at a site level and at the lowest granularity of the taxonomy.

The values in the screen can be changed in increment/decrement of 0.1 and the total value of the allocations should always match the total FTE count. If the user changes any allocation, a balance is shown in last column/row. Users need to balance out rows or columns completely to match the total on the top of the column. An error is shown to the user on the page and on the home page if the allocation is not balanced.

Also, allocation is restricted by levels such that a resource cannot be moved from “SSC” to “Non SSC”, similarly, a top down approach is followed in SSC, such that a resource cannot be moved from Global site to Regional site and from Regional site to Local site but vice versa can be done.

### Site allocation

The Site Allocation screen displays a detailed table for managing resource distribution across various sites. It includes columns for Baseline FTEs and Target FTEs, segmented into Non-SSC, Local, Regional, and Global categories. Users can view and adjust allocations for different functions such as Finance, HR, and IT. The interface allows toggling between 'In-house' and 'Outsourced' resources, with options to rename or create new scenarios. The table ensures that the total allocation for each site sums to 100%, facilitating precise resource planning and adjustments at a functional level.

The Site Allocation screen displays a detailed table for managing resource distribution across various sites. It includes columns for 'Country', 'Site code', and 'Baseline FTEs' with categories such as Non-SSC, Local, Regional, and Global. The 'Target FTEs' section allows users to adjust allocations for different sites, ensuring the total distribution equals 100% for each site. Users can toggle between 'In-house' and 'Outsourced' views. The interface supports scenario selection, with options to rename or create new scenarios, facilitating strategic planning and resource management at a functional level.

The site allocation screen is similar to the resource allocation screen but this is used to determine the target resource allocation on a function level for each site. By default . the allocations are predefined based from the “Site Selection” page. The user can change the distribution, however the total for each site (sum of Local, regional and global) must be equal to 100%.

This view is divided between the “Insource” and “Outsource” resources.

## One off cost assumptions

The one off cost assumptions are used to review the cost implications occurring from the allocations in site selection and site allocation pages. It is used to define the timelines and cost implications to implement the plan.

### Transition costs

The 'Transition costs' screen allows users to define and manage the expected period for implementing process improvements, divided into three phases: Ramp up, Pilot, and Hyper care. The interface includes a toggle for enabling detailed calculations and a dropdown to select scenarios, such as 'Restructuring, Q3 2025'. Users can input the duration of each phase in months, retention bonuses, and percentages of legacy and new team retention. The table displays the number of FTEs involved and calculates the transition costs for each phase, providing a total cost summary at the bottom. This screen aids in planning and budgeting for transition phases effectively.

The 'Transition costs' screen displays a detailed view for defining the expected period to implement process improvements across three phases: Ramp-up, Pilot, and Hyper care. It includes a table with columns for Function, Process, Sub-process, Start of transformation, Transition cost, and other metrics such as the number of months, percentage of legacy team retained, and number of FTEs created or retained. Users can enable detailed calculations and manage scenarios, with options to rename or create new ones. This screen helps users plan and calculate costs for each phase at a taxonomy level, based on assumptions from previous pages.

“transition costs” screen is used to define the expected period to implement the overall process improvement. The plan is divided into three phases. Time duration defined for these three phased forms the total duration of the process improvement plan. This screen is divided into a summary and detailed view. Summary view provides an aggregated view for the three phases. The detailed view provides the details for each phase at a taxonomy level. This view allows the user to plan each phase individually for each taxonomy levels. The costs in this view are calculated from the assumptions defined in the previous assumption pages.

### Severance costs

The 'Severance costs' screen displays a table summarizing severance cost assumptions for a selected scenario, 'Restructuring, Q3 2025'. Key UI elements include a toggle for enabling detailed calculations, options to rename or create new scenarios, and a table showing severance costs by phase. The table includes columns for the number of months of salary per impacted employee, the number of people impacted, and severance costs across different phases such as Ramp-up, Pilot, Hypercare, After care, and PI, with a total severance cost calculated. This screen helps users estimate and manage severance costs associated with resource allocation changes.

The 'Severance costs' screen displays a detailed table of severance cost assumptions for different countries and sites, segmented by phases such as Ramp-up, Pilot, Hypercare, After care, and PI. Key columns include '# months/years of service', 'Voluntary attrition', and 'Average years of service in baseline'. The table also shows the total severance costs in thousands of dollars for each location. Users can enable detailed calculations and manage scenarios with options to rename or create new ones. This screen helps users estimate and analyze severance costs associated with resource allocation changes across various phases.

“Severance costs” view provides the estimated severance to be paid in different phases due to the changes in resource allocation.

In the high level view , it is shown as an overall aggregated value while the detailed view shows the cost at a site level. However, only those sites are shown which are actually getting impacted.

The “Average years of service” column is populated from the baseline data. However if the years of service is not available then the user can provide the years from their end.

### Other one-off costs

The screen displays the 'Other one-off costs' assumptions interface within the Insights Cockpit. It features a table summarizing costs associated with employee setup and hiring, broken down by project phases such as Ramp-up, Pilot, Hypercare, and After care. The table includes columns for assumptions, costs per phase, and total costs. Users can toggle detailed calculations, select scenarios, and rename or create new entries. This view helps users define and analyze one-off employee-related costs during different implementation phases, providing both high-level and detailed insights.

The screen displays the 'Other one-off costs' section within the 'Shared Services' module, focusing on cost assumptions related to employees during the implementation phase. It features a table listing countries alongside columns for employee setup costs, hiring costs, and total costs. Users can view detailed calculations by enabling the toggle at the top. The scenario dropdown allows selection of different scenarios, such as 'Restructuring, Q3 2025'. Options to rename or create new scenarios are available. This interface helps users analyze and manage one-off employee-related costs at both a high-level and detailed country-specific view.

This view defines “Other” cost related to employees which are one off during the implementation phase. It has a “High level” view that shows the cost at an overall aggregated level. The detailed view shows the split of “other” costs at a country level and during each phase

## Output

The output screens provide the final calculate values based on the assumptions defined. All of these views are read only views and only for reporting purposes. No changes to the data or assumptions can be made from here. Filters implemented on any of the views will also be implemented on all the other Output views.

The 'SSOI: Analysis output' screen provides an overview of various reporting views related to cost optimization and workforce dynamics across multiple sites. It features six main cards: 'Savings and FTE evolution,' 'Annual run-rate savings,' 'FTE Impact overall view,' 'FTE Shifts,' 'One-time cost overview,' and 'Calendar view.' Each card includes a brief description and an arrow indicating navigation to detailed reports. This screen helps users access read-only reports for tracking savings, FTE evolution, and implementation impacts, with filters applied consistently across all views.

### Savings and FTE evolution

The 'Savings and FTE evolution overview' screen displays key financial metrics and workforce changes across sites. It includes dropdown filters for Function, Process, and Sub-process, allowing users to refine the data view. Key metrics such as Baseline Cost, Total Savings, Labor Arbitrage, Process Improvement, and One-off Cost are prominently shown. Below these metrics, a detailed table outlines the evolution of Full-Time Equivalents (FTEs), showing Baseline FTEs, FTEs at full potential, Net changes, Roles reduced, and Roles added. This screen helps users analyze cost savings and workforce adjustments, with filters that apply to subsequent screens.

This view provides an overall aggregated view of the savings in terms of money value and change in FTEs across sites. Filters from this page are carried forward to all the other screens.

### Annual run-rate savings

This view is a deep dive view for the money savings reported in the previous view. It provides breakdown of savings at a function level. Users can view the savings for In-house/outsourced or All resources.

The 'Annual run-rate savings' screen provides a detailed breakdown of financial savings at a functional level. It features dropdown filters for Function, Process, and Sub-process, allowing users to customize the view. The main section displays key metrics: Baseline cost, Total savings, Labor arbitrage, and Process Improvement, each with corresponding monetary values. A 'Functions breakdown' panel on the left lists various departments such as Finance, HR, IT, and more, with expandable subcategories showing specific savings figures. Users can toggle between In-house, Outsourced, and All resources to refine the data displayed, facilitating a comprehensive analysis of cost-saving measures.

### FTE impact overall view

The 'FTE impact overall view' screen provides a comprehensive analysis of Full-Time Equivalent (FTE) impacts across various functions. It includes dropdown filters for Function, Process, and Sub-process, allowing users to customize the data view. Key metrics displayed are Baseline FTE, FTE at full potential, Net changes, Roles reduced, and Roles added, with numerical values for each. A 'Functions breakdown' section lists specific FTE numbers for Finance, HR, IT, Legal, Facility Management, and Operations. This view helps users assess the current state of FTEs, including reductions and additions, facilitating strategic workforce planning.

The 'FTE impact overall view' screen provides a comprehensive analysis of Full-Time Equivalent (FTE) impacts across various functions. It includes dropdown filters for Function, Process, and Sub-process, allowing users to customize the data view. Key metrics displayed are Baseline FTE, FTE at full potential, Net changes, Roles reduced, and Roles added, each with corresponding numerical values. A 'Functions breakdown' section lists specific FTE numbers for Finance, HR, IT, Legal, Facility Management, and Operations. This view helps users assess the current FTE distribution and potential changes, facilitating strategic workforce planning.

This view is similar to the view before but reports on the current state of FTEs instead of the money value. This view also reports breakdown at taxonomy levels.

### FTE shifts

The “FTE shifts” view reports the changes in FTEs from the Baseline value to the Target value at taxonomy level for each site. The user can view the values at an aggregated level by site types or further drill down to a site level.

The 'FTE shifts' screen displays a detailed report of Full-Time Equivalent (FTE) changes from Baseline to Target values across various functions and processes. Users can filter data by Function, Process, and Sub-process using dropdown menus. The table presents Baseline and Target FTEs, segmented into Local, Regional, and Global categories, with expandable options for detailed views. The interface allows users to compare current and target staffing levels, facilitating analysis of workforce distribution across different site types. A 'Reset filters' option is available to clear selections and view all data comprehensively.

### One-time cost overview

This view provides a detailed view of the One time costs breakdown reported in the first view at a taxonomy level and split by cost types.

The One-time cost overview screen displays a detailed breakdown of costs at a taxonomy level, categorized by cost types such as Total one-time cost, Transition costs, Severance costs, and Additional one-time costs. Users can filter the data by Function, Process, and Sub-process using dropdown menus. The main panel shows the total costs in millions, with each category's specific costs listed below. A sidebar provides a functions breakdown, detailing costs for areas like Finance, HR, IT, Legal, Facility Management, and Operations. This view helps users analyze and manage one-time costs effectively within the platform.

### Calendar view

The calendar view allows the users to view the changes in FTEs and savings in each month of the process improvement implementation. The values are reported with breakdown at a taxonomy level. Users can view at the lowest granularity or at an aggregated level.

The Calendar view screen displays a detailed table showing changes in Full-Time Equivalents (FTEs) and savings across different months for process improvement implementations. Users can filter data by taxonomy and category, with options to view detailed evolution metrics such as Total # of FTEs, Gross Savings, Total one-off costs, and Net impact. The table is organized by months, allowing users to analyze data from month 1 to month 6, with a process summary column. Controls for adjusting the level of granularity and selecting the month range are available, facilitating detailed or aggregated data analysis.

### All data table

(Figma TBD)

This view provides a combined tabular view of the output values broken down at taxonomy level.

## Scenario management

SSOI calculations follow a top down approach. Changes made to an assumption or costs page has an impact on all the values in the pages coming after that page. However, there is no impact on assumptions/costs before that page. This has an impact on how the scenarios are managed for SSOI.

Scenarios in SSOI are global scenarios i.e a scenario create don one page is available on other pages as well. By default there will be a “Master” scenario which will have the original values and can not be edited.

Ex: If user creates a scenario “Scenario 1” on Benchmarking page , then when user moves to the “Site selection” page, they will see “scenario 1” already selected there. This scenario will be based on the values from the “scenario 1” on Benchmarking page. Similarly “Scenario 1” would be created for all the other pages in the sequence.

It includes columns for 'Country', 'SSC Sites', 'Type of SSC', 'Current FTEs', 'Average fully loaded cost ($k) / FTE', and 'Labor cost for new FTEs ($k) / FTE'. Users can view the average labor costs, which are read-only, and propose new labor costs for each site. The screen also features an 'Add location' button for creating new sites. The interface supports scenario selection, with options to rename or create new scenarios, facilitating detailed cost management and compliance with benchmarking standards.

The 'Site Selection' screen displays a data table where users can manage labor costs for various SSC sites. Key columns include 'Country', 'SSC Sites', 'Type of SSC', 'Current FTEs', and 'Average fully loaded cost ($K) / FTE'. Users can propose new labor costs for different departments such as Finance, HR, IT, and more, under 'Labor Cost for new FTEs ($K) / FTE'. The table allows users to expand or collapse department details. The scenario dropdown at the top lets users select different scenarios, and options to rename or create new scenarios are available. This screen aids in defining and adjusting labor costs at site levels for benchmarking purposes.

“Site Selection” screen can be used by the users to define the cost of labor at each site. They can define this at a site level or drill down to the lowest granularity level and define the labor cost at that level.

The default view shows the average labor cost for each site calculated from the baseline data. The “Average labor cost” is a read only column and cannot be edited by the user as it is the actual average labor cost from baseline data. However, user can propose a new labor cost for each site in the “Labor cost for new FTEs” column.

Users can also propose creation of new sites to comply with the benchmarking standards. However, this view is connected to the benchmarking view. If there is at least one % >0 in local or regional or global, user must have at least one equivalent site (e.g., one local if at least one process as a >0 in the previous, etc.). An error message would be shown to the user if the above condition is not met.

The 'Site Selection' screen allows users to manage labor costs for various sites. It displays a table with columns for 'Country', 'SSC Sites', 'Type of SSC', 'Current FTEs', 'Average fully loaded cost ($K) / FTE', and 'Labor cost for new FTEs ($K) / FTE'. Users can view average labor costs, which are read-only, and propose new labor costs for each site. The interface includes options to add new locations and manage scenarios, such as 'Restructuring, Q3 2025'. The screen supports compliance with benchmarking standards by allowing the creation of new sites if necessary.

### Resource allocation

The screen displays the 'Resource Allocation' interface within the 'Overall Assumptions' section, allowing users to adjust resource allocations by site. It features a table with columns for site code, type of site, baseline, target, and balance. Users can modify allocations in increments of 0.1, ensuring the total matches the full-time equivalent (FTE) count. A warning message highlights overstaffed or understaffed categories in red, prompting adjustments. The interface includes options to rename or create new scenarios, and a reset button to revert changes. The balance column shows discrepancies that need correction to maintain allocation consistency.

This view is an optional view. This is available only if the user selects the toggle in the Benchmarking view. It allows the user to change the resource allocation by site. It can be done only at a site level and at the lowest granularity of the taxonomy.

The values in the screen can be changed in increment/decrement of 0.1 and the total value of the allocations should always match the total FTE count. If the user changes any allocation, a balance is shown in last column/row. Users need to balance out rows or columns completely to match the total on the top of the column. An error is shown to the user on the page and on the home page if the allocation is not balanced.

Also, allocation is restricted by levels such that a resource cannot be moved from “SSC” to “Non SSC”, similarly, a top down approach is followed in SSC, such that a resource cannot be moved from Global site to Regional site and from Regional site to Local site but vice versa can be done.

### Site allocation

The Site Allocation screen displays a detailed table for managing resource distribution across various sites. It includes columns for Baseline FTEs and Target FTEs, segmented into Non-SSC, Local, Regional, and Global categories. Users can view and adjust allocations for different functions such as Finance, HR, and IT. The interface allows toggling between 'In-house' and 'Outsourced' resources, with options to rename or create new scenarios. The table ensures that the total allocation for each site sums to 100%, facilitating precise resource planning and adjustments at a functional level.

The Site Allocation screen displays a detailed table for managing resource distribution across various sites. It includes columns for 'Country', 'Site code', and 'Baseline FTEs' with categories such as Non-SSC, Local, Regional, and Global. The 'Target FTEs' section allows users to adjust allocations for different sites, ensuring the total distribution equals 100% for each site. Users can toggle between 'In-house' and 'Outsourced' views. The interface supports scenario selection, with options to rename or create new scenarios, facilitating strategic planning and resource management at a functional level.

The site allocation screen is similar to the resource allocation screen but this is used to determine the target resource allocation on a function level for each site. By default . the allocations are predefined based from the “Site Selection” page. The user can change the distribution, however the total for each site (sum of Local, regional and global) must be equal to 100%.

This view is divided between the “Insource” and “Outsource” resources.

nodes.title: SSOI Benchmarks
nodes.text:
### SSOI Benchmarks

Benchmarks page is the first page in the SSOI Assumptions module. It is a compulsory input page which a user needs to interact with.

The SSOI Benchmarks page displays a table for managing benchmark data and process improvement metrics within the SSOI Assumptions module. The table includes columns for 'Function/Process/Sub-Process', 'SSC Full Potential', 'SSC Benchmark' with Local, Regional, and Global percentages, and 'Customize Site Allocation' with toggle switches. The 'Process Improvement' section shows 'Process Improv. Max Potential', 'Client Starting Point', and 'Process Improv. Applied'. Users can edit benchmark values, ensuring the sum of Local, Regional, and Global equals 100%. The 'Reset to Default' option allows reverting changes. This page is essential for inputting and adjusting benchmark data for analysis.

The first columns is the “Taxonomy” column which will show the L1/L2/L3 in a tree data structure.

The column will only show the taxonomy levels selected by the user for the SSOI analysis even if benchmark data is available in the database for other levels. Benchmark data will be pre uploaded by COE team from backend. This is a common data for all cases, however case specific changes can be made by users.

The page has two sections - Benchmark data and Process Improvement data.

Benchmark section shows the actual values from the Benchmark file. These values are editable but validation will be done that the sum of Local, Regional and Global columns always should be equal to 100%. If a user has defined a custom taxonomy , then the row is shown as empty and the cells are highlighted with red borders

For the PI section, the “client starting point” is set to 3 by default. This point can be changed by the user with range from 1-5 (1 being the least mature and 5 – most mature)

“Enabled Site Allocation” column is a toggle column which can be used to provide details about a certain site. If a user toggles this ON for any site then the site gets displayed in the Resource allocation page where further details can be provided.

If a user attempts to leave page without resolving any error, following error should be shown:

The screenshot displays an error message dialog indicating that the percentages for Local, Regional, and Global must sum to 100%. It prompts the user to adjust the numbers accordingly. The dialog includes a 'See error info' link for more details, and two buttons: 'Copy error info' for copying the error details, and 'Got it!' to acknowledge the message. This screen is part of the SSOI Benchmarks page, ensuring users correct input errors before proceeding, maintaining data integrity in the benchmarking process.

If they still choose to Save then we show a warning sign on the Assumptions landing page.

On clicking Got It, user does not navigate away, they still need to stay on the page to fix the issue.

“Reset to Default” : This resets the benchmark to its last saved status. Eevery row also has individual “Rest to default” icons, clicking on these icons resets any changes made to that row.

nodes.title: One off cost assumptions
nodes.text:
## One off cost assumptions

The one off cost assumptions are used to review the cost implications occurring from the allocations in site selection and site allocation pages. It is used to define the timelines and cost implications to implement the plan.

### Transition costs

The 'Transition costs' screen allows users to define and manage the expected period for implementing process improvements, divided into three phases: Ramp up, Pilot, and Hyper care. The interface includes a toggle for enabling detailed calculations and a dropdown to select scenarios, such as 'Restructuring, Q3 2025'. Users can input the duration of each phase in months, retention bonuses, and percentages of legacy and new team retention. The table displays the number of FTEs involved and calculates the transition costs for each phase, providing a total cost summary at the bottom. This screen aids in planning and budgeting for transition phases effectively.

The 'Transition costs' screen displays a detailed view for defining the expected period to implement process improvements across three phases: Ramp-up, Pilot, and Hyper care. It includes a table with columns for Function, Process, Sub-process, Start of transformation, Transition cost, and other metrics such as the number of months, percentage of legacy team retained, and number of FTEs created or retained. Users can enable detailed calculations and manage scenarios, with options to rename or create new ones. This screen helps users plan and calculate costs for each phase at a taxonomy level, based on assumptions from previous pages.

“transition costs” screen is used to define the expected period to implement the overall process improvement. The plan is divided into three phases. Time duration defined for these three phased forms the total duration of the process improvement plan. This screen is divided into a summary and detailed view. Summary view provides an aggregated view for the three phases. The detailed view provides the details for each phase at a taxonomy level. This view allows the user to plan each phase individually for each taxonomy levels. The costs in this view are calculated from the assumptions defined in the previous assumption pages.

### Severance costs

The 'Severance costs' screen displays a table summarizing severance cost assumptions for a selected scenario, 'Restructuring, Q3 2025'. Key UI elements include a toggle for enabling detailed calculations, options to rename or create new scenarios, and a table showing severance costs by phase. The table includes columns for the number of months of salary per impacted employee, the number of people impacted, and severance costs across different phases such as Ramp-up, Pilot, Hypercare, After care, and PI, with a total severance cost calculated. This screen helps users estimate and manage severance costs associated with resource allocation changes.

The 'Severance costs' screen displays a detailed table of severance cost assumptions for different countries and sites, segmented by phases such as Ramp-up, Pilot, Hypercare, After care, and PI. Key columns include '# months/years of service', 'Voluntary attrition', and 'Average years of service in baseline'. The table also shows the total severance costs in thousands of dollars for each location. Users can enable detailed calculations and manage scenarios with options to rename or create new ones. This screen helps users estimate and analyze severance costs associated with resource allocation changes across various phases.

“Severance costs” view provides the estimated severance to be paid in different phases due to the changes in resource allocation.

In the high level view , it is shown as an overall aggregated value while the detailed view shows the cost at a site level. However, only those sites are shown which are actually getting impacted.

The “Average years of service” column is populated from the baseline data. However if the years of service is not available then the user can provide the years from their end.

### Other one-off costs

The screen displays the 'Other one-off costs' assumptions interface within the Insights Cockpit. It features a table summarizing costs associated with employee setup and hiring, broken down by project phases such as Ramp-up, Pilot, Hypercare, and After care. The table includes columns for assumptions, costs per phase, and total costs. Users can toggle detailed calculations, select scenarios, and rename or create new entries. This view helps users define and analyze one-off employee-related costs during different implementation phases, providing both high-level and detailed insights.

The screen displays the 'Other one-off costs' section within the 'Shared Services' module, focusing on cost assumptions related to employees during the implementation phase. It features a table listing countries alongside columns for employee setup costs, hiring costs, and total costs. Users can view detailed calculations by enabling the toggle at the top. The scenario dropdown allows selection of different scenarios, such as 'Restructuring, Q3 2025'. Options to rename or create new scenarios are available. This interface helps users analyze and manage one-off employee-related costs at both a high-level and detailed country-specific view.

This view defines “Other” cost related to employees which are one off during the implementation phase. It has a “High level” view that shows the cost at an overall aggregated level. The detailed view shows the split of “other” costs at a country level and during each phase

nodes.title: Output
nodes.text:
## Output

The output screens provide the final calculate values based on the assumptions defined. All of these views are read only views and only for reporting purposes. No changes to the data or assumptions can be made from here. Filters implemented on any of the views will also be implemented on all the other Output views.

The 'SSOI: Analysis output' screen provides an overview of various reporting views related to cost optimization and workforce dynamics across multiple sites. It features six main cards: 'Savings and FTE evolution,' 'Annual run-rate savings,' 'FTE Impact overall view,' 'FTE Shifts,' 'One-time cost overview,' and 'Calendar view.' Each card includes a brief description and an arrow indicating navigation to detailed reports. This screen helps users access read-only reports for tracking savings, FTE evolution, and implementation impacts, with filters applied consistently across all views.

### Savings and FTE evolution

The 'Savings and FTE evolution overview' screen displays key financial metrics and workforce changes across sites. It includes dropdown filters for Function, Process, and Sub-process, allowing users to refine the data view. Key metrics such as Baseline Cost, Total Savings, Labor Arbitrage, Process Improvement, and One-off Cost are prominently shown. Below these metrics, a detailed table outlines the evolution of Full-Time Equivalents (FTEs), showing Baseline FTEs, FTEs at full potential, Net changes, Roles reduced, and Roles added. This screen helps users analyze cost savings and workforce adjustments, with filters that apply to subsequent screens.

This view provides an overall aggregated view of the savings in terms of money value and change in FTEs across sites. Filters from this page are carried forward to all the other screens.

### Annual run-rate savings

This view is a deep dive view for the money savings reported in the previous view. It provides breakdown of savings at a function level. Users can view the savings for In-house/outsourced or All resources.

The 'Annual run-rate savings' screen provides a detailed breakdown of financial savings at a functional level. It features dropdown filters for Function, Process, and Sub-process, allowing users to customize the view. The main section displays key metrics: Baseline cost, Total savings, Labor arbitrage, and Process Improvement, each with corresponding monetary values. A 'Functions breakdown' panel on the left lists various departments such as Finance, HR, IT, and more, with expandable subcategories showing specific savings figures. Users can toggle between In-house, Outsourced, and All resources to refine the data displayed, facilitating a comprehensive analysis of cost-saving measures.

### FTE impact overall view

The 'FTE impact overall view' screen provides a comprehensive analysis of Full-Time Equivalent (FTE) impacts across various functions. It includes dropdown filters for Function, Process, and Sub-process, allowing users to customize the data view. Key metrics displayed are Baseline FTE, FTE at full potential, Net changes, Roles reduced, and Roles added, with numerical values for each. A 'Functions breakdown' section lists specific FTE numbers for Finance, HR, IT, Legal, Facility Management, and Operations. This view helps users assess the current state of FTEs, including reductions and additions, facilitating strategic workforce planning.

The 'FTE impact overall view' screen provides a comprehensive analysis of Full-Time Equivalent (FTE) impacts across various functions. It includes dropdown filters for Function, Process, and Sub-process, allowing users to customize the data view. Key metrics displayed are Baseline FTE, FTE at full potential, Net changes, Roles reduced, and Roles added, each with corresponding numerical values. A 'Functions breakdown' section lists specific FTE numbers for Finance, HR, IT, Legal, Facility Management, and Operations. This view helps users assess the current FTE distribution and potential changes, facilitating strategic workforce planning.

This view is similar to the view before but reports on the current state of FTEs instead of the money value. This view also reports breakdown at taxonomy levels.

### FTE shifts

The “FTE shifts” view reports the changes in FTEs from the Baseline value to the Target value at taxonomy level for each site. The user can view the values at an aggregated level by site types or further drill down to a site level.

The 'FTE shifts' screen displays a detailed report of Full-Time Equivalent (FTE) changes from Baseline to Target values across various functions and processes. Users can filter data by Function, Process, and Sub-process using dropdown menus. The table presents Baseline and Target FTEs, segmented into Local, Regional, and Global categories, with expandable options for detailed views. The interface allows users to compare current and target staffing levels, facilitating analysis of workforce distribution across different site types. A 'Reset filters' option is available to clear selections and view all data comprehensively.

### One-time cost overview

This view provides a detailed view of the One time costs breakdown reported in the first view at a taxonomy level and split by cost types.

The One-time cost overview screen displays a detailed breakdown of costs at a taxonomy level, categorized by cost types such as Total one-time cost, Transition costs, Severance costs, and Additional one-time costs. Users can filter the data by Function, Process, and Sub-process using dropdown menus. The main panel shows the total costs in millions, with each category's specific costs listed below. A sidebar provides a functions breakdown, detailing costs for areas like Finance, HR, IT, Legal, Facility Management, and Operations. This view helps users analyze and manage one-time costs effectively within the platform.

### Calendar view

The calendar view allows the users to view the changes in FTEs and savings in each month of the process improvement implementation. The values are reported with breakdown at a taxonomy level. Users can view at the lowest granularity or at an aggregated level.

The Calendar view screen displays a detailed table showing changes in Full-Time Equivalents (FTEs) and savings across different months for process improvement implementations. Users can filter data by taxonomy and category, with options to view detailed evolution metrics such as Total # of FTEs, Gross Savings, Total one-off costs, and Net impact. The table is organized by months, allowing users to analyze data from month 1 to month 6, with a process summary column. Controls for adjusting the level of granularity and selecting the month range are available, facilitating detailed or aggregated data analysis.

nodes.title: Scenario management
nodes.text:
## Scenario management

SSOI calculations follow a top down approach. Changes made to an assumption or costs page has an impact on all the values in the pages coming after that page. However, there is no impact on assumptions/costs before that page. This has an impact on how the scenarios are managed for SSOI.

Scenarios in SSOI are global scenarios i.e a scenario create don one page is available on other pages as well. By default there will be a “Master” scenario which will have the original values and can not be edited.

Ex: If user creates a scenario “Scenario 1” on Benchmarking page , then when user moves to the “Site selection” page, they will see “scenario 1” already selected there. This scenario will be based on the values from the “scenario 1” on Benchmarking page. Similarly “Scenario 1” would be created for all the other pages in the sequence.

tree.text:
# Backlog

## Advanced

Level selection for non existent levels for an assessment: If a user decides to chose a level that does not exist for an assessment, they would get a popup saying the level doesn exist for the assessment and the user needs to define the level hierarchy and provide relevant data

## Good to have

### User roles

Define roles like Admin, COE User and Consultants having various levels of access. Admin can have access to any case. COE users will have access to a limited no of cases and consultants will have access to only their current active case

### Admin page

Page for admin users to control access of users, add/remove/modify users. Delete/wipe old case data. Access to usage logs for the app
